title: 〜ようだ・〜みたいだ
description: 〜ようだ and 〜みたいだ both mean "it seems / it appears," a conjecture drawn from your own observation. They also form similes — "like a…" — with 〜みたいだ being the casual spoken version of the more formal 〜ようだ.

content.text: Use 〜ようだ or 〜みたいだ to state what seems to be true based on evidence you've personally taken in and reasoned about. 〜ようだ attaches to the plain form; a noun needs の (病気（びょうき）のようだ) and a な-adjective needs な (静（しず）かなようだ). 〜みたいだ is the casual equivalent and attaches directly — noun and な-adjective take nothing extra (病気（びょうき）みたいだ, 静（しず）かみたいだ).

content.japanese: plain form ＋ ようだ ／ みたいだ　（noun: の＋ようだ ／ noun＋みたいだ）
誰（だれ）かが部屋（へや）にいるようだ。
content.english: It seems someone is in the room.

content.japanese: 明日（あした）は雨（あめ）が降（ふ）るみたいです。
content.english: It looks like it's going to rain tomorrow.

content.japanese: 彼（かれ）は風邪（かぜ）をひいたみたいだ。
content.english: He seems to have caught a cold.

content.japanese: この道（みち）は間違（まちが）っているようだ。
content.english: It seems this road is the wrong one.

content.text: Both words also make comparisons — "like a ~." Use noun ＋ みたい or noun ＋ の ＋ よう to say one thing resembles another. To describe how an action is done ("treated them like numbers"), use the adverb forms 〜みたいに and 〜ように.

content.japanese: noun ＋ みたい ／ noun ＋ の ＋ よう　＝ like a 〜　（adverb: 〜みたいに ／ 〜ように）
カステラはデザートみたいでした。
content.english: The castella was like a dessert.

content.japanese: あの会社（かいしゃ）は社員（しゃいん）を番号（ばんごう）みたいに扱（あつか）っていました。
content.english: That company treated its employees like numbers.

content.japanese: 彼女（かのじょ）は天使（てんし）のように優（やさ）しい。

content.text: ようだ vs みたいだ

The meaning is the same — the difference is register. 〜ようだ is more formal and common in writing and careful speech; 〜みたいだ is casual and everyday. There's also the even more formal 〜のようだ for writing. When you observe and reason for yourself, ようだ／みたいだ fit; when you're relaying pure hearsay, use そうだ instead.

examples.japanese: plain form ＋ ようだ ／ みたいだ　（noun: の＋ようだ ／ noun＋みたいだ）
誰（だれ）かが部屋（へや）にいるようだ。
examples.english: It seems someone is in the room.

examples.japanese: 明日（あした）は雨（あめ）が降（ふ）るみたいです。
examples.english: It looks like it's going to rain tomorrow.

examples.japanese: 彼（かれ）は風邪（かぜ）をひいたみたいだ。
examples.english: He seems to have caught a cold.

examples.japanese: この道（みち）は間違（まちが）っているようだ。
examples.english: It seems this road is the wrong one.

examples.japanese: noun ＋ みたい ／ noun ＋ の ＋ よう　＝ like a 〜　（adverb: 〜みたいに ／ 〜ように）
カステラはデザートみたいでした。
examples.english: The castella was like a dessert.

examples.japanese: あの会社（かいしゃ）は社員（しゃいん）を番号（ばんごう）みたいに扱（あつか）っていました。
examples.english: That company treated its employees like numbers.

examples.japanese: 彼女（かのじょ）は天使（てんし）のように優（やさ）しい。

headings.text: ようだ vs みたいだ
